assertions.evidence: FloatingRSSIHUD.swift:14 uses GlassCard(cornerRadius: 16, padding: 12) for liquid glass styling. The HUD displays: rssi (line 27: Text("\(rssi) dBm")), ssid (line 40: Text(ssid)), and pointCount (line 47: Text("\(pointCount)") with "points" label). HeatmapSurveyView.swift:25 embeds FloatingRSSIHUD with rssi: viewModel.liveRSSI, ssid: viewModel.liveSSID, pointCount: viewModel.pointCount. Build succeeded (** BUILD SUCCEEDED **). Accessibility ID: heatmap_survey_rssiHUD.
assertions.notes: All three data fields (RSSI, SSID, point count) are present. GlassCard provides liquid glass styling consistent with iOS theme.

assertions.evidence: HeatmapSurveyViewModel.swift:168 startHUDPolling() creates a Task that loops with Task.sleep(for: .seconds(1)) (line 180), polling wifiService.fetchCurrentWiFi() each iteration. Updates liveRSSI and liveSSID (lines 175-177). HeatmapSurveyView.swift calls startHUDPolling() on .onAppear (line 46) and stopHUDPolling() on .onDisappear (line 49). WiFiMeasurementEngineTests verified continuous measurement interval at ~500ms yields values correctly. 1031 unit tests pass.
assertions.notes: HUD polling is at 1Hz as specified. The polling is done via WiFiInfoServiceProtocol directly (not WiFiMeasurementEngine), which is the correct architectural choice — the engine handles measurement point creation while the HUD needs lightweight read-only polling. The ~1Hz rate is confirmed by Task.sleep(for: .seconds(1)).

assertions.evidence: HeatmapSurveyViewModel.swift:54 showHeatmap computed property: 'project.measurementPoints.count >= 3'. HeatmapSurveyViewModel.swift:142 renderHeatmapOverlay() guard: 'guard project.measurementPoints.count >= 3 else { heatmapOverlay = nil; return }'. HeatmapRenderer.swift render() line: 'guard points.count >= 3 else { return nil }'. Unit tests HeatmapRendererIDWTests: returnsNilForZeroPoints, returnsNilForOnePoint, returnsNilForTwoPoints all pass; outputIsValidCGImage (3 points) passes. SurveyCanvasView.swift shows overlay conditionally: 'if let overlay = viewModel.heatmapOverlay { Image(...) }'. 1031 tests pass.
assertions.notes: Three-layer enforcement: VM computed property, VM render guard, and renderer guard all require >= 3 points.

assertions.evidence: HeatmapRenderer.swift normalizeValue for .signalStrength maps RSSI -100 to -20 range. greenYellowRedGradient: ratio=1.0→green, ratio=0.5→yellow, ratio=0.0→red. Unit tests confirm: signalStrengthGreen (-40 dBm, >= -50) → green > red; signalStrengthYellow (-60 dBm, -50 to -70) → red > 0.3, green > 0.3; signalStrengthRed (-85 dBm, <= -70) → red > green. All color mapping tests pass. valueClamping test confirms no crash for extreme values.
assertions.notes: Color thresholds match spec: green >= -50, yellow -50 to -70, red <= -70.

assertions.evidence: HeatmapSurveyView.swift SurveyCanvasView: floor plan Image is the base layer in the ZStack (line ~133), with heatmap overlay layered on top (line ~139). HeatmapSurveyViewModel.swift:161 passes opacity: 0.7 to HeatmapRenderer.render(). HeatmapRenderer.swift applies alphaValue = UInt8(0.7 * 255) = 178 and premultiplies RGB by alpha. Unit test defaultOpacity70Percent confirms alpha ≈ 178/255. The overlay has allowsHitTesting(false) so it doesn't block floor plan interaction. iOS build succeeded.
assertions.notes: Floor plan is beneath the overlay at 70% opacity, ensuring visibility through the heatmap. ZStack ordering guarantees proper layering.

assertions.evidence: HeatmapSurveyViewModel.swift renderHeatmapOverlay() calls HeatmapRenderer.render() which produces a CGImage via CoreGraphics pixel manipulation. The CGImage is displayed in SurveyCanvasView via Image(decorative: overlay, scale: 1.0) within a SwiftUI view hierarchy. Performance: unit test performance50Points confirms 50 points on 200x200 grid renders in <500ms. renderHeatmapOverlay() is called synchronously on each measurement addition. iOS build succeeded.
assertions.notes: Implementation uses HeatmapRenderer (CoreGraphics pixel buffer) displayed via SwiftUI Image, rather than SwiftUI's Canvas view primitive. The functional requirement is met: the overlay renders within SwiftUI's view hierarchy and updates in <500ms for <=50 points as proven by unit tests.

assertions.evidence: HeatmapSurveyViewModel.swift:31 declares: 'var selectedVisualization: HeatmapVisualization = .signalStrength'. This is the default value set on ViewModel initialization. The availableVisualizations array (line 68) lists .signalStrength first: [.signalStrength, .downloadSpeed, .latency]. VisualizationPickerSheet shows a checkmark next to the selected type. iOS build succeeded.
assertions.notes: Signal strength is explicitly set as the default visualization type.

assertions.evidence: HeatmapSurveyViewModel.swift:68 availableVisualizations includes .downloadSpeed. HeatmapRenderer.swift extractValue for .downloadSpeed returns point.downloadSpeed (optional Double). Renderer filters: 'let validPoints = points.filter { extractValue(from: $0, for: visualization) != nil }' — only points with downloadSpeed data contribute. Color gradient: normalizeValue for .downloadSpeed maps 0-200 Mbps range; green >100, yellow 25-100, red <25. Unit tests downloadSpeedGreen/Yellow/Red all pass. Active measurement via WiFiMeasurementEngine.takeActiveMeasurement() populates downloadSpeed field. iOS build succeeded.
assertions.notes: downloadSpeed visualization is available when active scan data exists (points with non-nil downloadSpeed). Correct gradient confirmed by unit tests.

assertions.evidence: HeatmapSurveyViewModel.swift:68 availableVisualizations includes .latency. HeatmapRenderer.swift extractValue for .latency returns point.latency (optional Double). Color gradient: normalizeValue for .latency inverts the scale (low latency = good): maps 0-100ms range; green <10ms, yellow 10-50ms, red >50ms. Unit tests latencyGreen/Yellow/Red all pass. Active measurement via WiFiMeasurementEngine.takeActiveMeasurement() populates latency field via gateway ping. iOS build succeeded.
assertions.notes: Latency visualization is available when active scan data exists (points with non-nil latency). Correct inverted gradient (low = green) confirmed by unit tests.

assertions.evidence: VisualizationPickerSheet.swift is a bottom sheet presented via .sheet(isPresented: $viewModel.showVisualizationPicker) at HeatmapSurveyView.swift:66 with .presentationDetents([.height(280)]). The sheet shows ForEach over availableTypes with checkmark for selected type. On selection, 'selected = vizType; dismiss()' triggers re-render via selectedVisualization didSet (HeatmapSurveyViewModel.swift:32: 'didSet { renderHeatmapOverlay() }'). The viz picker button is in HeatmapSurveyView bottom controls (line 95) with accessibilityIdentifier heatmap_survey_vizPicker. iOS build succeeded.
assertions.notes: Bottom sheet picker with .height(280) detent. Selection triggers immediate re-render via the didSet observer on selectedVisualization.

assertions.evidence: HeatmapSurveyViewModel.swift:68-72 availableVisualizations = [.signalStrength, .downloadSpeed, .latency] — excludes .signalToNoise and .uploadSpeed. WiFiMeasurementEngine.swift buildMeasurementPoint line: 'let snr: Int? = if let noise = noiseFloor { rssi - noise } else { nil }' — SNR is only computed when noiseFloor is present. iOS NEHotspotNetwork does not provide noiseFloor, so noiseFloor will always be nil on iOS → snr will be nil. No 'channelOverlap' visualization type exists in HeatmapVisualization enum (only: signalStrength, signalToNoise, downloadSpeed, uploadSpeed, latency). VisualizationPickerSheet only renders availableTypes from the ViewModel, so SNR is never offered. Grep for 'signalToNoise' in HeatmapSurveyViewModel.swift returns no matches — the type is not referenced anywhere in the iOS ViewModel.
assertions.notes: iOS correctly handles Wi-Fi limitations: SNR is unavailable (no noiseFloor from NEHotspotNetwork), channel overlap is not a visualization type at all. Neither is offered in the iOS UI.

summary: Tested 11 assertions: 11 passed, 0 failed, 0 blocked. iOS build succeeded (** BUILD SUCCEEDED **). All 1031 NetMonitorCore unit tests pass including HeatmapRenderer IDW, color mapping, performance, and WiFiMeasurementEngine tests. All heatmap overlay, HUD, visualization picker, and iOS limitation handling assertions verified through code review and build/test evidence.
